solution: Since \( |z^{n}| = |z|^{n} \), first find \( |z| = \sqrt{([[a]])^{2}+([[b]])^{2}} \), then raise it to the \( [[n]] \)-th power (an even power, so the result is a clean integer): \( \left|z^{[[n]]}\right| = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This multiplies the modulus by \( n \) instead of raising it to the \( n \)-th power.
This raises \( |z|^{2} = a^{2}+b^{2} \) to the \( n \)-th power, squaring the correct answer instead of matching it.
This raises each part separately and adds, instead of computing the modulus first.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This doubles the correct value.

stem: Let \( z = [[r]]\left(\cos\dfrac{[[k]]\pi}{6} + i\sin\dfrac{[[k]]\pi}{6}\right) \). By De Moivre's theorem, \( z^{[[n]]} = r^{[[n]]}\left(\cos(n\theta) + i\sin(n\theta)\right) \). Find the principal argument \( \arg\left(z^{[[n]]}\right) \) (in radians, in \( (-\pi, \pi] \)).
solution: By De Moivre's theorem, multiplying the argument by \( n \) (then reducing to the principal range \( (-\pi,\pi] \)) gives \( \arg\left(z^{[[n]]}\right) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the raw multiple \( n\theta \) without reducing it to the principal range \( (-\pi,\pi] \).
This is \( \arg(z) \) alone, forgetting to multiply by \( n \) as De Moivre's theorem requires.
This has the wrong sign, as if the angle were measured clockwise.
This adds an extra half turn to the correct angle.
This subtracts an extra half turn from the correct angle.
This adds an extra quarter turn to the correct angle.

stem: Let \( z = [[r]]\left(\cos\dfrac{[[k]]\pi}{6} + i\sin\dfrac{[[k]]\pi}{6}\right) \). Using De Moivre's theorem, write \( z^{[[n]]} \) in rectangular form \( x + yi \).
solution: By De Moivre's theorem, \( z^{[[n]]} = [[r]]^{[[n]]}\left(\cos\dfrac{[[n]][[k]]\pi}{6} + i\sin\dfrac{[[n]][[k]]\pi}{6}\right) \), which simplifies to the rectangular value \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This raises the modulus to the \( n \)-th power but forgets to multiply the angle by \( n \) inside the trig functions.
This multiplies the angle inside cosine by \( n \) but leaves the sine's angle and the modulus's power unchanged.
This is the complex conjugate of the correct value, as if the sign of the angle were flipped.
This negates the correct value (a sign slip in applying De Moivre's theorem).
This is off by one in the real part.
This adds an extra unit to the imaginary part.

stem: Let \( z_{1} = [[r1]]\left(\cos\dfrac{[[k1]]\pi}{6} + i\sin\dfrac{[[k1]]\pi}{6}\right) \) and \( z_{2} = [[r2]]\left(\cos\dfrac{[[k2]]\pi}{6} + i\sin\dfrac{[[k2]]\pi}{6}\right) \). Compute \( \left|\dfrac{z_{1}}{z_{2}}\right| \).
solution: Since \( \left|\dfrac{z_1}{z_2}\right| = \dfrac{|z_1|}{|z_2|} = \dfrac{r_1}{r_2} \), and the arguments do not affect the modulus, \( \left|\dfrac{z_1}{z_2}\right| = \dfrac{[[r1]]}{[[r2]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This multiplies the moduli instead of dividing them.
This inverts the ratio, dividing \( r_2 \) by \( r_1 \) instead of \( r_1 \) by \( r_2 \).
This is off by one from the correct ratio.
This is off by one from the correct ratio.
This subtracts the moduli instead of dividing them.
This adds the moduli instead of dividing them.

stem: Let \( z_{1} = [[r1]]\left(\cos\dfrac{[[k1]]\pi}{6} + i\sin\dfrac{[[k1]]\pi}{6}\right) \) and \( z_{2} = [[r2]]\left(\cos\dfrac{[[k2]]\pi}{6} + i\sin\dfrac{[[k2]]\pi}{6}\right) \). Find the principal argument \( \arg\left(\dfrac{z_{1}}{z_{2}}\right) \) (in radians, in \( (-\pi, \pi] \)).
solution: Since \( \arg\left(\dfrac{z_1}{z_2}\right) = \arg(z_1) - \arg(z_2) \), subtract the angles \( \dfrac{[[k1]]\pi}{6} - \dfrac{[[k2]]\pi}{6} \) and reduce to the principal range: \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This adds the two angles instead of subtracting them.
This subtracts in the wrong order, giving \( \arg(z_2) - \arg(z_1) \) instead of \( \arg(z_1) - \arg(z_2) \).
This has the wrong sign for the correct principal value.
This adds an extra half turn to the correct angle.
This subtracts an extra half turn from the correct angle.
This adds an extra quarter turn to the correct angle.

stem: Let \( z_{1} = [[r1]]\left(\cos\dfrac{[[k1]]\pi}{6} + i\sin\dfrac{[[k1]]\pi}{6}\right) \) and \( z_{2} = [[r2]]\left(\cos\dfrac{[[k2]]\pi}{6} + i\sin\dfrac{[[k2]]\pi}{6}\right) \). Using \( z_{1}z_{2} = r_{1}r_{2}\left(\cos(\theta_{1}+\theta_{2}) + i\sin(\theta_{1}+\theta_{2})\right) \), write \( z_{1}z_{2} \) in rectangular form \( x + yi \).
solution: Multiply the moduli, \( r_{1}r_{2} = ([[r1]])([[r2]]) \), and add the arguments, \( \dfrac{[[k1]]\pi}{6}+\dfrac{[[k2]]\pi}{6} \), then convert to rectangular form: \( z_{1}z_{2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This adds \( z_1 \) and \( z_2 \) instead of multiplying them.
This multiplies the moduli but mismatches the cosine and sine angles instead of using the sum \( \theta_1+\theta_2 \) in both.
This is the complex conjugate of the correct product.
This negates the correct product (a sign slip).
This is off by one in the real part.
This adds an extra unit to the imaginary part.
